coredata.dc:title: Chromium(Ⅵ) removal in a solar-driven photocatalytic fuel cell with carbon quantum dots modified p-type zinc-based metal−organic framework photocathode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Meng
Du, Hao
Fu, Yangjie
Ma, Xin
Li, Ningyi
Hao, Derek
Wang, Qi
coredata.dc:description: High-efficiency photoelectrode played a significant role in photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) systems. In this study, a zinc-based metal-organic framework modified by carbon quantum dots (N-Zn-MOF/CQDs) and the BiVO4 were respectively presented as the photocathode and photoanode of the PFC system toward Cr(Ⅵ) reduction. Various analytical techniques were utilized to characterize the physicochemical, optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples. With the addition of CQDs, the visible-light response and charge separation rate were remarkably enhanced. In the PFC system, the circuit current density of 58.04 mA cm−2 and max power density of 1.35 mW cm−2 were achieved, which was superior to other reported PFC systems. Under optimized conditions, 100% Cr(Ⅵ) can be removed within 120 min of visible-light illumination. This work provided a new method to decorate the zinc-based metal−organic framework cathode to dramatically improve the photocatalytic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P-type metal−organic framework
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic fuel cell
Photocathode
Cr(Ⅵ) reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sustainability of Cr(VI) removal in PFC

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of CQDs, N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 and PFC, (b) XRD patterns of BiVO4, (c) Zn-MOF, N-Zn-MOF and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs, SEM patterns of (d) BiVO4, (e) N-Zn-MOF, (f) SEM patterns and (g) EDS images of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of N-Zn-MOF and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs: for (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) Zn 2p.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) UV-Vis-DRS and (c) solid-state emission spectra of N-Zn-MOF and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2.

(a) The transient current density curve and (b) the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs photocathode. The VOC-t curves of BiVO4|Pt, Pt|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (c) and BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (d); JSC-t curves of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (e), BiVO4|Pt and Pt|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (f) with 0.1 M KH2PO4 pH = 7.
LSV (a) and polarization curves (b) of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs.

Cr(VI) reduction dynamics (a) and (b) by different systems (pH unadjusted). The effect of pH (c) and corresponding rate constants (d) by optimized BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs under visible light. The effect of different initial Cr(VI) concentrations (e) and applied potential (f) on the reduction of Cr(VI).
The repeated cycles of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 and BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF PFC systems.

(a) Mott-Schottky plot and (b) XPS-VB spectrum of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2, (c) effect of trapping agent on reduction experiments of Cr(VI), (d) DMPO- •O2 − under different conditions; (e) proposed mechanism for charge separation in of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2.

head.ce:title: Chromium(Ⅵ) removal in a solar-driven photocatalytic fuel cell with carbon quantum dots modified p-type zinc-based metal−organic framework photocathode

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou 310018, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-efficiency photoelectrode played a significant role in photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) systems. In this study, a zinc-based metal-organic framework modified by carbon quantum dots (N-Zn-MOF/CQDs) and the BiVO4 were respectively presented as the photocathode and photoanode of the PFC system toward Cr(Ⅵ) reduction. Various analytical techniques were utilized to characterize the physicochemical, optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples. With the addition of CQDs, the visible-light response and charge separation rate were remarkably enhanced. In the PFC system, the circuit current density of 58.04 mA cm−2 and max power density of 1.35 mW cm−2 were achieved, which was superior to other reported PFC systems. Under optimized conditions, 100% Cr(Ⅵ) can be removed within 120 min of visible-light illumination. This work provided a new method to decorate the zinc-based metal−organic framework cathode to dramatically improve the photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution and energy shortage are facing us [1–3]. Up to now, many techniques have been studied for wastewater treatment [4–6], such as microbial reduction, chemical reduction and carbon adsorption [7,8], while most of these methods require a lot of energy [9,10]. In recent years, recovering energy while treating wastewater has attracted considerable interesting around the world. Therefore, it is extremely essential to realize energy conversion and environmental pollution purification. Photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) can deal with pollutants and simultaneously generate electricity. It integrates the advantages of photocatalysis [11,12], photoelectrocatalysis [13,14], fuel cells and solar cells [15], which both treat sewage and recover chemical energy [16,17]. For instance, Kaneko et al. constructed the PEC system with TiO2 photoanode and Pt cathode [18]. In this system, NH3 as “fuel” was decomposed in a photoanode and the H2 was produced in a cathode under ultraviolet-light irradiation. However, low photon utilization efficiency and the high cost restrict the application of this system. Semiconductor materials are still the focus of PFC development. Recently, many researchers used different methods to improve the photoanodes' performance [19]. For example, Wang et al. [20] prepared CdS quantum-dot-sensitized TiO2 photoanode to enhance PFC activity, and the results exhibited that the solar-light harvesting ability of photoanode was significantly improved with the introduction of CdS quantum dots. In addition, Xia et al. [16] fabricated a visible-light BiVO4/WO3/W heterojunction. They observed that a natural connection between WO3 and W substrate facilitated the transfer of electrons to BiVO4. The optimized BiVO4/WO3/W photoanode could obtain a photocurrent of 2.01 mA cm−2, which was 1.8 and 2.05 times higher than that of WO3 and BiVO4 photoanode, respectively. Moreover, semiconductor materials with excellent charge transmission performance as the cathode also contributed to the construction of the highly effective PFC [21].

One of the major challenges in PFC is the high cost of Pt cathode [12,22]. Hence many researchers tried to develop photocathode with high PFC performance and cost effectiveness instead of Pt cathode [23,24]. The dual-functional PFC was researched for efficient wastewater treatment and electricity generation. The inherent bias between photoanode and photocathode contributes to the separation of the photo-generated electron–hole pairs [17]. As one of alternative cathodes, cuprous oxide (Cu2O) could respond to visible light. For instance, Li et al. designed a dual-functional PFC with TiO2/Ti photoanode and Cu2O/Cu photocathode for wastewater treatment and electricity generation [25]. Regretfully, in this system, Cu2O [26] did not show superior stability because of the photocorrosion.

Therefore, designing highly effective and stable cathodes with visible-light response is urgent [27]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of porous materials with high surface areas, and have been widely investigated in separations [28], gas storage [29] and catalysis [30,31]. Compared with inorganic materials, organic ligands exhibit a higher optical absorption, thereby effectively improving the light utilization efficiency of MOFs [32]. However, due to the low conductivity of MOFs, there are few reports on MOFs in photoelectric chemistry. To date, many strategies had been adopted to modify the MOFs. Adding electron reservoirs is a promising method to enhance the photoactivities of the photocatalysts. Due to their low toxicity, photostability, and biocompatibility, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are regarded as to be excellent electron donors and acceptors and can be used as charge transfer channels of PEC devices [33,34]. Much more attention has been paid to their application in photocatalysis owing to their outstanding electron-transfer rates [35,36].

Herein, in the PFC system, a novel CQDs modified p-type zinc-based metal−organic framework (N-Zn-MOF/CQDs) fabricated via a one-step solvothermal method and BiVO4 were respectively employed as the photocathode and photoanode. The constructed PFC showed excellent photocatalytic performance toward Cr(VI) reduction. Under light irradiation, the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 system showed good stability with slight attenuation of activity. Various parameters including Cr(Ⅵ) concentration, pH value, and applied voltage were systematically studied. The charge transfer mechanism was also revealed.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), glycolic acid (C2H4O3) and potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. Zinc nitrate hydrate (Zn(NO3)2·9H2O) and poly(vinyl alcohol)1799 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. They were analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared by using high-purity deionized water and provided in the Supplementary Materials.
BiVO4 photoanodes were obtained via the two-step calcination processes [37]. The detailed preparation process can be referred to Scheme S1 in the supporting information.

The precursor solution containing 1 M glucose and 1 M NaOH was ultrasonically treated for 2 h. Then, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 with 3 M HCl. The same volume of ethanol was added to the above solution with stirring for 1 h. Thereafter, the original solution was dialyzed using a semi-permeable membrane (MWCO 1000) to remove impurities [38].

First, the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glasses (2 × 5 cm2, 7 Ω cm−2) were ultrasonically washed with acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 15 min, respectively. The N-Zn-MOF/CQDs photoanodes were prepared according to a modified method. Typically, 2.68 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.68 g of glycolic acid (C2H4O3) were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol and stirred for 20 min. Then, various amounts of CQDs suspension (0, 0.5, 1, 2 or 3 mL) were added into the above solution. Subsequently, the mixture was treated at 160 °C for 16 h. The resulting N-Zn-MOF/CQDs film electrodes were washed with ethanol several times and dried at 80 °C for 12 h [39]. The resulting samples were labeled as N-Zn-MOF, N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-0.5, N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-1, N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2, and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-3.

An X-ray diffraction (XRD) system (D/Max-2550 PC, Regaku, Japan) was used to obtain the XRD patterns of film electrodes. The surface morphologies and microstructures of film electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, sigma 300, Zeiss, German) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, America). The surface element and chemical state of the photoelectrodes were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, America). The UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-vis DRS) of the photoelectrodes were measured by a Shimadzu spectrophotometer (UV-2012 PC, Unico, China). The organic group of photoelectrodes was measured by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, VERTEX-70, Bruker, German). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Japan). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were measured by EMXplus (Bruker, German).

The photoelectrochemical measurements of the photoelectrodes were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation in a standard three-electrodes system, where the BiVO4 photoanode and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode were used as working electrodes, Pt sheet and Ag/AgCl was applied as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The photocurrent tests were carried out at 0.2 V and −0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode in 0.1 M Na2SO3–Na2SO4 mixed solution under visible light (λ > 420 nm) which was provided by a 300 W Xenon lamp (CEL-300, Beijing China Education Au-light Co., Ltd). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with the frequency ranging from 1 to 100 kHz at the open-circuit potential. The Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots were achieved in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution under dark conditions. The BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system was connected in series with the electrochemical workstation to measure the PFC performance. The open circuit voltage (VOC) was achieved in 0.1 M KH2PO4 solution at pH 7. As well, the short-circuit current density (J SC) was obtained in the same solution when the voltage was zero.

The BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system was illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). As observed, the two electrodes were placed in parallel in the 50 mL solution (0.1 M KH2PO4 and 40 μM Cr(Ⅵ), and the ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) was utilized as hole scavenger. The distance between the lamp and the photoelectrodes is about 15 cm. Prior to reaction, the PFC system was kept in dark for 30 min to reach absorption/desorption equilibrium. Samples were taken out every 30 min, and the residual concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) was determined by the 1,5-diphenylarbazide coloration method at the absorption wavelength of 545 nm.

The crystalline structures of the as-prepared film electrodes were investigated by XRD. In Fig. 1(b), the diffraction peak at 18.5°, 18.9°, 28.9°, 30.5°, 34.5°, 35.2° and 42.4° were respectively assigned to the (110), (011), (121), (040), (200), (002) and (051) facets of BiVO4 (PDF#48-0744). For N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (Fig. 1(c)), the diffraction peak at 11.8°, 17.2°, 33.3°, and 34.3° agreed well with simulated N-Zn-MOF [39] (PDF#44-0558), and the peak at 17.7° corresponds to CQDs, which was also evidenced by TEM (Fig. S1). However, compared with the N-Zn-MOF, the intensity of peaks at 11.8°, 17.2°, 33.3°, and 34.3° in N-Zn-MOF/CQDs were significantly weakened, which may be ascribed to the loss of ordered structures within the N-Zn-MOF framework caused by the CQDs decoration [38]. No additional peaks were presented in the film electrodes. Therefore, these XRD patterns demonstrate the successful construction of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs.

The surface morphologies and microstructures of the electrodes were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1(d), the BiVO4 photoelectrode was composed of numerous dense polygonal particles, and its surface was relatively smooth. From Fig. 1(e), the N-Zn-MOF loaded on the FTO presented a “crack mud” structure with multiple cracks. However, with the decoration of CQDs (Fig. 1(f)), the “crack mud” structure was not maintained, this composite gave an irregular block structure. Fig. S1 showed the TEM images of CQDs. As seen, the as-prepared CQDs consisted of carbon crystals with a distinct lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.324 nm corresponding to the CQDs (311) plane. Meanwhile, the Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) spectra and element mapping confirmed Zn, C, N and O were uniformly distributed in N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (Fig. 1(g)). Fig. S2(a) showed the TEM image of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2, which was in good agreement with the results of SEM. Moreover, an irregular porous elliptic structure was also observed. The mapping images proved the homogeneous distribution of Zn, N, O and C (Fig. S2(b)).

The surface element and chemical state of the electrodes were revealed by the XPS. The existence of C, O, N and Zn in N-Zn-MOF and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs was demonstrated (Fig. S3), which was in accordance with EDX results. For N-Zn-MOF samples, three peaks at 284.80, 286.30 and 288.40 eV were respectively assigned to the C-C, C-O and N-C=N bands (Fig. 2 (a)) [40]. Compared with N-Zn-MOF, the peaks corresponding to C-O and N-C=N bands in N-Zn-MOF/CQDs showed respectively positive and negative shifts, indicating the strong interaction between N-Zn-MOF and CQDs. Moreover, for N 1s spectra, the two peaks at 399.60 and 402.8 eV also moved respectively to the right and left (Fig. 2(c)). The peak centered at 402.3 eV corresponded to the amino group (-NH2) [41]. The area ratio of N 1s peaks in -NH2 was increased from 0.069 to 0.079 with the addition of CQDs, suggesting a substantial increase in the number of -NH2. This could promote charge separation efficiency and thus enhance the visible-light absorption [42]. As for the Zn 2p spectrum of N-Zn-MOF in Fig. 2(d), the two peaks at 1021.98 and 1045.08 eV were respectively assigned to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of Zn2+ [43]. However, the O 1s (Fig. 2(b)) spectra and Zn 2p (Fig. 2(d)) spectra slightly shifted, which may be the result of the interaction between N-Zn-MOF and carbon CQDs [33]. The peak at 533.46–533.54 eV could be assigned to adsorbed water [44]. Furthermore, we also found that the two peaks of Zn 2p spectra shifted to the left. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the possibility that the carbonization of the ligand caused by CQDs increased the number of substances in the oxidation state, such as ZnO [45].

The above conclusion was confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3 (a)). As depicted, the characteristic peak at 1585.2 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H in N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2. The peaks in the range of 2500–2000 cm−1 were attributed to triple bond and the cumulative double bond, while the characteristic peaks disappeared with the addition of CQDs, which was assigned to the breakage of some double bonds. UV-vis DRS was used to assess the light absorption behavior of the prepared photoelectrodes. From Fig. 3(b), the N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 showed better light absorption response in the full spectrum in comparison with N-Zn-MOF, which was attributed to the synergy between N-Zn-MOF and CQDs. The results indicated that the N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 sample with broaden spectrum response exhibited more superior PEC performance. As shown in Fig. S7, the BiVO4 photoanode had a high absorption in the visible-light region. Moreover, the bandgaps of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode and BiVO4 photoanode were respectively 2.16 eV and 2.38 eV based on the Tauc/Davis-Mott expression (Eq. (1)) [46].
(1)
α h ν = A
(
h ν −
E g
)
n / 2
Where, α, h, ν, and A represents the absorption coefficient, plank constant, light frequency and proportionality, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-Zn-MOF significantly changed with the incorporation of CQDs, which also suggested the recombination of photoinduced carriers by CQDs was effectively inhibited (Fig. 3(c)).

The charge separation and migration processes were further studied by transient photocurrent responses at −0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl under visible-light illumination. As seen from Fig. 4 (a), no obvious photocurrent signal was observed in N-Zn-MOF photocathode. The N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode showed the highest photocurrent response. With the modification of the CQDs, N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 showed higher photo-generated charge separation efficiency compared with N-Zn-MOF. Furthermore, the interface charge transfer behavior of the photocathodes was also revealed by EIS. Commonly, the arc radii of Nyquist plots were inversely proportional to the transfer rate of photo-induced electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode presented the smallest Nyquist arc radius suggesting that the N-Zn-MOF/CQDs possessed a lower interfacial charge transfer resistance (Table S1). Hence these characterizations proved the introduction of CQDs accelerated the separation and transfer of photo-excited charge carriers, thus enhancing the PEC activity. Additionally, due to its outstanding photoelectrochemical properties demonstrated by the EIS and photocurrent response (Fig. S4), the BiVO4 was chosen as the photoanode.

The open-circuit voltage (VOC) and short-circuit current (JSC) were measured to examine the photoelectric properties of the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system. As shown in Fig. 4(c), with the light on, the VOC rose rapidly to the maximum value for both BiVO4 and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2. In the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system, the VOC remarkably increased, indicating that this constructed system can be driven by interior bias (Fig. 4(d)). It can be seen from Fig. 4(e) and (f), the visible-light illumination facilitated the separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers of the BiVO4 photoanodes. BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system showed an obvious enhancement in short-circuit current, and the transient photocurrent reached 0.041 mA cm−2. These obtained results confirmed that the fabricated PFC system was conducive to Cr(VI) reduction.

The PFC systems with different photocathodes were fabricated to evaluate their performance. The polarization and power density curves were shown in Fig. 5 . The VOC by the PFC system with BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 was measured to be 0.4 V. As exhibited, the maximum J SC of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 system was 14.5 mA/cm2, which was higher than that of other PFC systems. Moreover, the maximum power density was achieved in these PFC systems. Accordingly, the PFC system equipped with Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 produced higher power density of 1.23 mW cm−2 according to Eq. (2) [47,48].
(2)
P =
J
S C
×
V
O C
A
Where JSC, VOC and A were respectively open circuit voltage (V), short-circuit current (mA) and the area covered by catalyst on the electrode surface (cm2). Hence the PEC enhancement of PFC with Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 was demonstrated.

The achieved results were compared with previous literatures. As seen in Table S2, this fabricated PFC system exhibited comparable performance with the largest Jsc of 14.5 mA cm−2 and Pmax of 1.23 mW cm−2 than previously reported PFC systems.

The constructed PFC systems with different photocathodes were then applied for the reduction of Cr(VI). As illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system displayed the highest reduction efficiency (40.6%) of Cr(VI) within 120 min of visible-light illumination, which was considerably higher than that of Pt|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 (0.07%) and BiVO4|Pt (13.2%). There is no obvious adsorption of Cr(VI) by the PFC system during the adsorption-desorption process in the dark. This acceleration in Cr(VI) reduction may be ascribed to the synergistic effect between BiVO4 and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2. Also, we can conclude that the Cr(VI) reduction performance over BiVO4 photoanode was enhanced with the combination of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2. Also, we can conclude that the Cr(VI) reduction performance over BiVO4 photoanode was enhanced with the combination of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2. Presumably, the positively-charged groups resulting from N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 can absorb Cr(VI) efficiently, thereby greatly promoting the reduction of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. S6(a), the pseudo-first-order kinetic model of the PFC systems was also analyzed. The rate constant of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 was 0.00459 min−1, which was about 3.6 and 6.9 times as high as that of BiVO4|Pt and Pt|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2, respectively. The synergistic effect was originated between BiVO4 photoanode and N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode because of the obtained results confirmed the superiority of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system. When the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PEC system was established, the Cr(VI) reduction rates increased (Fig. S5), indicating the combination of this photoanode (BiVO4) and photocathode (N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2) synergistically promoted the separation of photo-generated electron/hole pairs under visible-light illumination.

While, the photocatalytic activities of all N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-X photocathodes were showed in Fig. 6(b), N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode displayed highest photocatalytic performance. And the pseudo-first order model can further indicate that N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 exhibited best photocatalytic ability (Fig. S6(b)).

pH value can alter the physio-chemical properties of Cr(VI) in the solution, thus affecting its reduction efficiency. Typically, in neutral and acidic environments (pH 2.0–6.0), Cr(VI) species existed in the form of Cr2O7 2− and HCrO4 −, and under alkaline conditions (pH > 6) Cr(VI) species presented in the form of CrO4 2− (Fig. S7). Generally, the reduction of Cr(VI) at different pH values followed Eqs. (3) and (4). And the thermodynamic driving force for Cr(VI) reduction decreases by 79 mV with an increase of pH per unit [49]. Fig. 6(c) showed the effects of pH on Cr(VI) reduction efficiency in BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC systems. The adsorption of Cr(VI) in this system was increased with the decrease in pH. At highly acidic pH, the negatively charged Cr(VI) was attracted to the positively charged surface of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs. As found, Cr(VI) can be completely removed at pH 2, while only 20.3% of Cr(VI) were reduced at pH 8 within 120 min. This was due to the consumption of photogenerated electrons promoted by the abundant H+. And the first-order kinetic model (Fig. 6(d)) also proved this conclusion.
(3)
14

+
C r

O 7
2 −

(4)

O H
)
3
+ 5 O
H −

The effects of initial concentration of Cr(VI) on reduction efficiency were also investigated. From Fig. 6(e), with increasing the initial concentration of Cr(VI) from 20 to 40 μmol L−1, the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) decreased. When the reaction conditions were fixed, the number of the photogenerated electron (e−) migrating to N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 was constant. Thus, when the Cr(VI) concentration descended from 40 μmol L−1 to 20 μmol L−1, the reduction rate increased [50]. To explore the role of inherent bias in the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC systems, the reduction of Cr(VI) in different applied external potentials were also performed under the same conditions, and the results were shown in Fig. 6(f). The removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was only 21% at an applied potential of 0.3 V within 120 min. Nevertheless, when the applied potential was increased to 0.5 V, the removal rate of Cr(VI) reached 45%. The reason is that the external bias promoted the separation of photo-induced charge carriers and accelerated the transfer of electrons to the photocathode, thus enhancing the reduction of Cr(VI). Therefore, the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) at the bias provided by the BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC systems was comparable with that at 0.5 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sustainability of Cr(VI) removal in PFC
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusability of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 was extremely important for its practical applications. Therefore, the cyclic experiments were conducted toward Cr(VI) reduction. As shown in Fig. 7 , no noticeable deactivation of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system was observed after five cycles, suggesting the high stability of the constructed BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system. Conversely, owing to the unstable materials of N-Zn-MOF photocathode, significant decline was found for removal efficiencies of Cr(VI) after five cycles. Therefore, the photocathode composed of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs had better stability than N-Zn-MOF.

The Mott-Schottky (M − S) measurements were conducted to estimate the flat band potential (E fb) of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode and BiVO4 photoanode (Fig. S8). As noted, the M − S plots of N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode showed the negative slopes (Fig. 8 (a)), implying the typical p-type semiconductor characteristic. And its E fb was deduced to be ca. 1.71 V vs. Ag/AgCl. In order to further determine its energy band structure, XPS VB spectra measurements were carried out to obtain the VB maximum (Fig. 8(b)), and the calculated value was 2.37 eV. The contact-potential difference between the sample and the analyzer vs. NHE at a pH of 7 was calculated using the following Eq. (5) [51].
(5)
E
N H E
= Φ +
V B
max
− 4.44
Where ENHE, Φ, and VBmax represent the potential of the NHE (eV), the electron work function of the analyzer (4.2 eV), and the valence band maximum determined via valence band XPS (eV), respectively. The measured potentials vs. RHE were converted into the potentials vs. Ag/AgCl using the following Eq. (6) [52]:
(6)
E
N H E
=
E
A g / A g C l
+ 0.059 × p H
Where ERHE represents the converted potential vs. RHE and EAg/AgCl represents the measured potential vs. Ag/AgCl. The calculated result was 1.72 eV, which was consistent with the M − S plots.

Similarly, BiVO4 photoanode was a typical n-type semiconductor, and the conduction band (CB) position was 0.22 V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. S9). The self-provided bias between the PFC system was ensured by the flat band potentials of two electrodes, which can realize the electron migration from BiVO4 photoanode to N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode because of enough driving force.

The removal mechanism of Cr(VI) by BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 system was proposed based on the above results and discussion (Fig. 8(e)). Furthermore, trapping experiments were performed to determine the contribution of reactive species in the PFC system (Fig. 8(c)). EDTA-2Na and AgNO3 were used as scavengers to capture h+ and e−, and Nitrogen gas (N2) was used to remove dissolved O2 to prove the presence of •O2 − [53,54]. As shown in Fig. S10, with the addition of AgNO3, the rate constant greatly decreased from 0.00459 to 0.00181 min−1. The introduction of N2 also inhibited the Cr(VI) reduction. The EPR was provided to further evidence the existence of •O2 − (Fig. 8(d)). Therefore, the e− and •O2 − were the key active species toward Cr(VI) reduction. And the CQDs can absorb near-infrared light, and then emit shorter wavelength light (390–564 nm) as a result of up-conversion (Fig. S11) [38]. Based on the above results, two reduction pathways were proposed: (1) Cr(VI) was reduced by electrons resulting from the BiVO4 photoanode; (2) Cr(VI) was absorbed over the N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode due to the positively charged nitrogen-containing function groups and then reduced by electrons from N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 photocathode. The two pathways can synergisticly improve the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency in the PFC system.

Overall, BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs PFC system was successfully constructed for Cr(Ⅵ) reduction and electric production, where N-Zn-MOF/CQDs and BiVO4 as were respectively adopted as the photocathode and photoanode. The introduction of CQDs can broaden the light response range and accelerate the separation of photo-generated charge carriers. Under neutral conditions, the obtained VOC, Pmax and JSC in the PFC system were 0.4 V, 1.35 mW cm−2 and 58.04 mA cm−2, respectively, superior to most of the reported PFC systems, and 40.6% Cr(Ⅵ) was removed by BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs-2 PFC system. 100% of Cr(Ⅵ) could be removed within 120 min under the optimized conditions. The outstanding reduction activity of Cr(Ⅵ) was retained after five cycling experiments. This work offers a novel insight for the construction of BiVO4|N-Zn-MOF/CQDs PFC system for the treatment of Cr(Ⅵ)-containing wastewaters and energy conversion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21876154 and 22276168), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (JRK22001), and and “Xinmiao” Talents Program in Zhejiang Province (2022R408C081). The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www. shiyanjia.com) for the XRD and XPS tests.
